transcript.clean_text: We’re back with stage 5 of the Tour de France right here on GCN Racing - don’t forget to subscribe and hit the bell icon so that you get notified every time we upload a video. A look back at yesterday. A fast and frantic finale into Nancy resulted in a big bunch sprint. Deceuninck Quickstep gave everybody a lesson in leadouts, perfectly delivering Elia Viviani to the finish line, giving them two stage wins in two days. They also maintained the overall race lead with Alaphilippe, whilst Sagan held on to green by 23 points, and Van Aert remains the best young rider. Today, though, almost certainly won’t be for the pure bunch sprinters. Stage 5 is 175kms from Saint dié des Vosges to Colmar, and while there may be a flat finish, before that the riders have to deal with 2 3rd category and 2 second category climbs. Once again we had ideal weather conditions in the North East of France, and today, with the uncertainty of how the stage would pan out, there were plenty of riders interested in making the day’s breakaway. We had multiple attacks in the opening few kilometres of the stage, and as you might expect, Thomas de Gendt of Lotto Soudal was amongst the main protagonists. This was the third move he’d made, this time with Benoit Cosnefroy and Jan Tratnik, however, he’d miss out on the actual move. That one was started by Mads Wurst Schmidt of Katusha Alpecin, a team rumoured to be stopping at the end of this year. He was later joined by Simon Clarke of EF Education First, Latvian Champion Toms Skujins of Trek Segafredo, and finally, De Gendts teammate and current King of the Mountains, Tim Wellens. 4 very strong riders, and as such, although their lead rapidly rose initially, the bunch was not going to let them too far up the road. 2 minutes was their advantage when Bora Hansgrohe started working on the front. Wellens added an extra 2 points to his KOM tally on the first climb of the day, the Cote de Grendelbruch. The next major point of interest was the intermediate sprint in Heiligenstein, Clarke took maximum points up front, but it was behind that we had the big battle, Viviani once again getting the better of Sagan and Matthews, reducing Sagan’s lead to 22 points in that competition. A short while later we reached the first 2nd category climb of this year’s race. The Cote de Haut Koenigsbourg, 5.9kms at 5.8%. The peloton at this point still being lead by Bora Hansgrohe, although they’d now been joined by Team Sunweb. The gap to the leading quartet was down to 1m35 as Wellens took the maximum 5 points on offer at the top of the climb. It’s been a Tour to forget so far for Tony Gallopin, a crash on day one, another yesterday, and today, he was brought to a stop by a rogue parasol. Hopefully the sun will shine on him at some point during this race. Deceuninck Quickstep also added their firepower to the chase - nobody at this point was a threat to their yellow jersey, but they were out for a third straight stage win. With the gap to the leaders tumbling, Skujins decided it was time to do a Wellens, attacking on the penultimate climb of the day, the Cote des trois Épis, and going on a solo mission out front. His gap at the top of that climb, 19 seconds over Wellens, 42 over Simon Clarke, and a little over 1 minute over the bunch. A daredevil descent ensued for the Latvian, but despite that, his gap continued to fall. Just a couple of kilometres later, Wellens and Clarke were caught by what was left of the main peloton. And then, on the final climb of the day, the inevitable happened, Skujins was brought back into the fold by a group that was flying up the climb courtesty of the pace being set by Team Sunweb’s Wilco Keldermann. And that pace gave us our first big GC casualty, Ilnur Zakarin has not looked anything like the man that he was at the Giro d’Italia in May, and it looks like it’ll be stage wins that he’ll be going for in the mountains of the Tour. Meanwhile Boasson Hagen was doing his best to fight back on after an untimely mechanical. Xandro Meurissen of Wanty Gobert accelerated over the top to take the maximum KOM points on the final climb of the day, but after that, it was a battle amongst the GC riders and teams to get a good position at the front for the impending descent. This man can’t help himself can he?! Alaphilippe just loves to race his bike, uphill and down, it was he who briefly lead the reduced bunch down the descent before allowing Sunweb to once again take over. Amazingly, having fallen a minute behind the group, the Boss did finally manage to get back on, being helped over that last part by teammate Michael Valgren. The question was, how much would that chase have taken out of his legs. Trying to prevent it being a sprint was this man, former world Champion Rui Costa of UAE Team Emirates attacked with 7kms to go, quickly getting a 10 second gap, and without many domestiques left to do the chasing behind, it looked like he had a chance. Bora Hansgrohe were alert to the danger though, sending a man to the front to help Sunweb. With 2kms to go Costa’s dream was over, German Champion Max Schachmann leading the group under the flamme rouge, but there were about 10 riders still believing in their chances of a stage win. Matej Mohoric lead through the final corner with 450m to go, next up it was Daryl Impey, leading out Matteo Trentin. Greg Van Avermaet behind that, Sagan Looming large behind that, Van aert and Colbrelli a little caught out at this point. Sagan launched his sprint with 180m to go, swinging over to the right hand side of the road, Trentin doing everything he could to get on terms, but failing, and van Aert just having to come from too far back, Sagan, winner of stage 5. Peter Sagan. What more can you say about him?! That marks his 12th Tour stage win, his 43rd top 3, his 70th top 10. The man is a phenomenon, and his lead in the points competition is now up to Classifications. Alaphilippe remains the overall race leader, but his lead is down to 14 seconds on Van Aert who picked up 6 bonus seconds on the line. The rest of the top 8 is unchanged. Sagan moves himself up 9 places in the GC, to 9th, whilst Greg Van Avermaet falls one place to 10th. Sagan now has a commanding lead in the Green Jersey competition, 47 points in front of Michael Matthews, with Vivani a further 5 points back in 3rd. Van Aert meanwhile has extended his lead in the young riders classification. Tomorrow, we hit the mountains. It’s the first big test for the general classification riders as they head from Mulhouse to La Planche des Belles Filles. Of the 7 classified climbs, 2 are 3rd cat, 2 are 2nd cat, and 3, including the final one to the finish line, are 1st category. Organisers have introduced an extra sting in the tail at the end of the Belles Filles, a gravel sector that pitches up to 24%. It’s sure to have a major effect on the GC. I’ll be back tomorrow to bring you all the action. In the three previous editions where this climb has been used, the leader after the stage has been the rider that’s gone on to win overall. Have no doubt, this is a big one which you don’t want to miss. Come back tomorrow, same time, same place, for highlights of stage 6, or if you want to watch it live in Europe, Eurosport will have it from flag drop to finish line.
